title: 1 Kings
author: Unknown; Jewish tradition attributes it to Jeremiah
setting: The last days of David, Solomon's glorious reign, and the division of the kingdom into Israel (north) and Judah (south) following Solomon's death, through Ahab and Elijah.
purpose: To show that Israel's history is governed by covenant faithfulness: obedience brings blessing, idolatry brings judgment. The division of the kingdom is the direct consequence of Solomon's unfaithfulness.
themes: Elijah the prophet and the contest on Carmel
Temple worship vs. idolatry at Bethel and Dan

outline.label: The Divided Kingdom: Rehoboam to Ahab

before.label: David's reign and the Davidic covenant

after.label: 2 Kings — fall of Israel and Judah

key_people.name: Solomon
key_people.role: David's son and Israel's wisest king; his construction of the temple is his crowning achievement, but his foreign wives lead his heart astray and split the kingdom

key_people.name: Rehoboam
key_people.role: Solomon's son whose foolish response to the people's plea triggers the division of the kingdom into Israel and Judah

key_people.name: Jeroboam
key_people.role: First king of the northern kingdom who immediately installs golden calves at Bethel and Dan, setting the idolatrous pattern every subsequent northern king follows

key_people.name: Elijah
key_people.role: The great prophet of fire who confronts Ahab, kills the prophets of Baal at Carmel, and flees in despair to Horeb — one of the most psychologically vivid figures in the OT

key_people.name: Ahab and Jezebel
key_people.role: Israel's most wicked king and his Phoenician queen, who together introduce Baal worship systematically into Israel and persecute the prophets of YHWH

context: 1 Kings spans roughly 970–853 BC, beginning with Solomon's accession and ending with Ahab's death. The book was compiled during or after the Babylonian exile to explain why God allowed his people to be deported — the answer given is the systematic covenant unfaithfulness of the kings. The division of the kingdom after Solomon's death is the pivotal catastrophe, directly attributed to Solomon's apostasy and Rehoboam's folly.
christ_connection: Solomon's wisdom and his construction of the temple both point to Christ: Jesus says 'something greater than Solomon is here' (Matt 12:42), and John identifies Jesus' body as the true temple (John 2:21). Elijah's ministry — confronting apostasy, raising the dead, being fed by God in the wilderness — prefigures John the Baptist (Matt 11:14) and anticipates Christ's own ministry. The temple as God's dwelling place finds its ultimate fulfilment in the Incarnation and the indwelling of the Spirit.

key_verses.note: Solomon's request for a discerning heart to govern God's people — the prayer that pleases God and becomes the model for seeking wisdom rather than wealth or power
'But will God indeed dwell on the earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you' — Solomon's temple prayer acknowledging that no structure can contain the infinite God
God's verdict on Solomon: because he has turned away from the covenant, the kingdom will be torn from his son — sin has dynastic consequences
Elijah's challenge at Carmel: 'How long will you go limping between two different opinions?' — the demand for undivided covenant loyalty

themes_detail.title: Wisdom and Its Corruption
themes_detail.text: Solomon begins as the exemplar of divinely-given wisdom, and his early reign is the OT's closest approximation to the kingdom of God on earth — peace, justice, prosperity, nations coming to hear God's wisdom. But wisdom without ongoing humility and covenant faithfulness becomes the seedbed for pride. Solomon's 700 wives and 300 concubines are not merely moral failures; they represent systematic alliance-building with foreign nations whose gods he then tolerates and eventually worships. The tragedy is proportional: the wisest man fails in the most fundamental way.

themes_detail.title: The Temple as God's Dwelling Place
themes_detail.text: The construction and dedication of the temple (chs. 5–8) is the architectural and theological climax of the unified monarchy. God's glory filling the temple at its dedication (8:10–11) is a theophany that echoes the filling of the tabernacle (Exod 40:34–35), confirming that this is the place where heaven and earth meet. But Solomon's prayer carefully distinguishes between the temple as a place of prayer and God's transcendent reality: 'heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain you' (8:27). The temple is a signpost pointing to the greater reality of God's presence in Christ.

themes_detail.title: Covenant Obedience as the Condition of Blessing
themes_detail.text: God's promise to Solomon at Gibeon and in the temple dedicatory response (9:1–9) is conditional: blessing and dynasty are tied to covenant faithfulness; disaster and exile follow apostasy. 1 Kings is the first sustained demonstration of Deuteronomy's covenant curses playing out in history. The division of the kingdom, the prophets' opposition to the northern rulers, and the persistent idolatry of both kingdoms are all presented as the outworking of a broken covenant, not random political misfortune.

themes_detail.title: The Prophetic Witness Against Syncretism
themes_detail.text: Elijah's ministry (chs. 17–19; also ch. 21) is the book's prophetic counterweight to royal apostasy. The Mount Carmel contest (ch. 18) is a dramatic theological argument: YHWH answers by fire; Baal does not answer at all. But the aftermath — Elijah's flight and despair — reveals that even prophetic victory does not produce lasting national repentance. The still small voice at Horeb (19:12) recalibrates Elijah's understanding: God's work proceeds not through spectacular power alone but through the patient preservation of a faithful remnant.
